output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.8, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.6, "R1.7": 0.7}}

Critique with recommended revisions. The section reads cleanly but rests on several methodological and rhetorical choices that an experienced reviewer will flag immediately. The issues below are ordered roughly by severity.

**1. The 50/50 evaluation ratio is not "balanced practice" — it is unrepresentative and inflates headline metrics.**
A 50% prior for spoof attacks is wildly mismatched to any plausible deployment prior on Prolific/Lucid (where the base rate of synthetic respondents is presumably small — 1%? 5%?). Accuracy and F1 at a 50/50 prior are not informative about deployment performance; at a 1% spoof prior, even AUROC=0.987 can correspond to a precision well under 50% at usable recall. The recommendation that "future benchmarks adopt the balanced 50/50 protocol" actively entrenches a misleading convention. Replace this with:
- Report EER and t-DCF (the standard ASVspoof metrics).
- Report a DET curve, or at least operating-point precision/recall at multiple realistic spoof priors (e.g., 1%, 5%, 10%).
- Soften or remove the protocol recommendation.

**2. "State of the art" is unsupported by the evidence presented.**
The only comparator is a re-implemented RawNet2. There is no comparison to AASIST, RawGAT-ST, Whisper-based detectors, or SSL-frontend baselines (e.g., WavLM/XLS-R + AASIST), all of which are stronger and more recent than RawNet2 on ASVspoof-style tasks. With a single, dated baseline, the strongest defensible claim is "outperforms a RawNet2 baseline under matched training," not "new state of the art." Either add the missing baselines or downgrade the claim.

**3. The held-out partition appears to share generators with training, which inflates results.**
Listing the same four systems (VITS, YourTTS, XTTS-v2, FreeVC) for evaluation without stating that training used different generators (or different speaker/text splits) leaves the reader assuming in-distribution evaluation. The interesting question for a deployed detector is cross-generator generalization — performance on TTS systems unseen during training (e.g., ElevenLabs, Play.HT, Tortoise, OpenVoice). Add a leave-one-generator-out or unseen-attack evaluation, or explicitly disclose the train/test generator overlap.

**4. The threat model and data provenance are underspecified.**
- "Bona fide responses collected from a major panel provider" — which one? Under what consent? Any IRB/ethics statement?
- Are spoofed utterances passed through a realistic channel (codec, VoIP compression, mobile microphone playback-and-recapture)? Detectors that work on clean studio audio routinely collapse under telephony codecs and replay. The threat model specifies "voice channels on platforms such as Prolific Voice and Lucid" but the evaluation appears to be on uncompressed audio.
- Speaker overlap between bona fide and the speaker pool used by VC systems is not addressed.

**5. Architecture description is too thin to be reproducible.**
"A frozen Wav2Vec2-XLS-R front-end and a lightweight binary discriminator trained with cross-entropy" omits: which XLS-R variant (300M? 1B? 2B?), which layer's embeddings, pooling strategy, discriminator architecture and size, training data composition and size, optimizer, learning rate, epochs, augmentations, and seed/variance across runs. Single-seed numbers with no confidence intervals should be reported with ±std over ≥3 seeds.

**6. The deployment recommendation is premature.**
"Deploy AURIX at the accuracy-optimal threshold identified on our development partition" assumes the dev-set prior matches deployment — see issue (1). Threshold selection should target a deployment-relevant operating point (e.g., FPR ≤ 1% to keep false rejections of real respondents tolerable), not accuracy on a 50/50 partition.

**7. Minor prose and structure.**
- The opening repeats "voice" three times in two sentences; tighten.
- The per-attack breakdown is more informative than the aggregate and could be promoted; conversely the aggregate-then-breakdown ordering currently lets the headline number dominate.
- "Indicating consistent gains across both end-to-end TTS families and VC pipelines" overstates: you've shown consistent *absolute accuracy*, not consistent *gains*, since the RawNet2 per-attack numbers aren't reported. Either add them or rephrase.
- "Prior best system" is unjustified given only one baseline; say "the RawNet2 baseline."

**Suggested minimal rewrite of the results paragraph:**

> On the held-out partition AURIX attains EER of X.X% and t-DCF of Y.YY; at a 1% spoof prior the operating point achieving 1% FPR yields recall of Z%. Under matched training, our RawNet2 re-implementation attains EER of A.A%. Per-attack EERs are … . We were unable to compare against AASIST and SSL+AASIST baselines in this draft; that comparison is the principal limitation of the present evaluation.

Uncertainty: I have not verified that ASVspoof-style metrics (EER, t-DCF) are conventional in your specific subfield of survey-platform spoof detection — they are standard in speaker-verification anti-spoofing and almost certainly expected here, but if your venue has its own conventions, adapt accordingly. I have also assumed Prolific Voice / Lucid are real or representative platforms as stated; I did not independently verify their existence or audio-channel characteristics.
